title: First Quantum Mining And Operations Limited v Zubao Harry Juma (Appeal No. 22/2025) [2026] ZMSC 10 (17 April 2026)

appellant: First Quantum Mining And Operations Limited

paragraphs.text: Stanbic Bank Zambia Ltd v Natasha Patel - Appeal No. 274/2022
Midlands Milling Limited v Lloyd Tembo -Appeal No. 297 of 2022
Atlantic Bakery v ZESCO Ltd- Selected Judgment No. 61of 2018

Murray & Roberts Constructions Ltd v Lusaka Premier Health Ltd and
Another - SCZ Appeal No. 141/2019
Kingfred Phiri v Life Master Ltd - Appeal No. 24 of 2023

Anderson Kambole Mazoka & Others v Levy Patrick Mwanawasa &
Others (2005) Z.R. 138
Silumbwe v Barclays Bank Zambia Ltd- SCZ Judgment No. 40 of 2017

Zambia National Commercial Bank PLC v Geoffrey Muyamwa and 88
Others-SCZ Selected Judgment No. 37 of 2017
Legislation and works referred to:

Oxford Advanced Learners' Dictionary, 6th Edition by A.S Hornby, edited
by Sally Wehmeier, Oxford University Press 2000
J2
1 Introduction
1.1 This appeal, arises from an employment dispute that has traversed
the Industrial Relations Division of the High Court and the Court
of Appeal, culminating in the grant of leave to appeal by the Court
of Appeal, propelled by the profound legal uncertainty its own
conflicting decisions have caused.
1.2 The appellant, First Quantum Mining and Operations Limited,
seeks to impugn the judgment of the Court of Appeal delivered on
18th September 2024, which overturned the decision of the High
Court dismissing the respondent's claim for service benefits
following his summary dismissai from employment for gross
misconduct.
2 Background Facts
2.1 The respondent, Zubao Harry Juma, was initially employed by the
appellant on a fixed-term contract for 6 months commencing on
20th March, 2015 and lapsing on 19th September 2015 or upon
completion of the project for which he was engaged, whichever
event occurred first. At the end of the contract, the respondent was
entitled to one month's gross salary for each completed year of
service or pro rata; commutation of all or any outstanding leave
days; and payment of all outstanding shifts.
J3
2.2 By letter dated 14th September 2016, his fixed term contract of
employment which was to expire on 19th September 2016, was
renewed on a permanent basis effective 20th September 2016. All
the other terms and conditions remained unchanged.
2.3 Subsequently, on 1st November 2020, the appellant notified the
respondent of an impending termination of his employment by
reason of redundancy, effective 30th November 2020. The letter
also spelt out his redundancy package as 5 months' gross salary,
K4,000.00 repatriation, commutation of all outstanding leave
days, 13th cheque pro rata, and payment of all outstanding shifts,
less any monies owed to the company.
2.4 However, on 12th November 2020, before the redundancy could
take effect, the respondent was arrested by company security for
unauthorised removal of company property. Following disciplinary
proceedings, on 30th November 2020, where he admitted taking
company property without authority, he was summarily dismissed
from employment for gross misconduct effective forthwith.
2.5 The letter of summary dismissal indicated what he was entitled to,
namely payment of all outstanding shifts, and commutation of all
leave days, less any monies owed to the company.
J4
2.6 After an unsuccessful appeal, against what he termed a severe
decision, the respondent instituted proceedings in the Industrial
Relations Division of the High Court, challenging the fairness of
his dismissal and claiming, inter alia, "payment of service benefits
and accrued leave days." The affidavit in support of the complaint
cited the service benefits defined in the redundancy letter.
2.7 The appellant filed an answer and affidavit in support of the
answer, but did not attend the hearing of the matter. After
inquiring by the court, since the respondent appeared in person,
he confirmed that his employment commenced on 20th September
2016 and that he was relying on his affidavit evidence. The learned
trial judge (Mumba, J) dismissed the claim for unfair dismissal. He
found that the dismissal was substantively and procedurally fair.
2.8 On service benefits, the court held that having been dismissed for
cause, the respondent could not claim the redundancy benefits.
The trial court also found that the respondent had failed to lead
evidence specifying any other type of accrued service benefits he
was claiming, beyond the redundancy benefits. Thus, his claim
under this head failed. For accrued leave benefits, the court found
that he was entitled to the benefits notwithstanding his dismissal
from employment. Each party was ordered to bear own costs.
JS
2.9 Aggrieved by the decision declining to award him service benefits
for the reason that he had failed to lead evidence specifying any
other type of accrued service benefits he was claiming, and costs,
the respondent appealed to the Court of Appeal. His main ground
of appeal assailed the trial court's refusal to award him "service
benefits ... as contained in the redundancy termination letter
exhibited as 'ZJHl' as read with the letter of offer of employment
on a fixed term contract exhibited by the appellant as 'TCl'."
2.10 The Court of Appeal, in the impugned judgment, upheld the
appeal. While accepting that the respondent had failed to prove his
claim based on the redundancy letter, the Court suo motu
determined two new issues: a) that the respondent was entitled to
service benefits under his expired fixed-term contract, and b) that
the respondent was entitled to a severance package under section
54(l)(c) of the Employment Code Act, holding that "employees
engaged on a permanent basis are entitled to a severance package
under that section".
2.11 This interpretation of section 54(1)(c) by the Court of Appeal
directly contradicted its own recent decisions in Stanbic Bank
Zambia Limited v Natasha Patel1 and Midlands Milling Limited
v Lloyd Tembo2 The appellant, with the respondent's support,
.
J6
successfully obtained leave to appeal to this Court, recognising the
profound questions of public importance the matter presents.
3 Appeal to this Court
3.1 There are two grounds of appeal, framed relatively as follows:
3.1.1 The Court of Appeal erred in law when it, suo motu, fronted a
ground of appeal anchored on the provisions of section 54{1){c) of
the Employment Code Act, an issue that was not the subject of the
claim before the High Court when the appellant had consistently,
albeit erroneously, canvased for a redundancy package.

l.2The Court of Appeal erred in fact and law when contrary to its
holding in the cases of Stanbic Bank Zambia Limited v Natasha
Pate11 and Midlands Milling (2011) Limited v Lloyd Temb02, it
opined that in its interpretation of the above provision, employees
engaged on a permanent basis are entitled to a severance package
under section 54(1)(c) of the Employment Code Act.
3.2 In support of ground 1, counsel for the appellant contends, in brief,
that the Court of Appeal broke a foundational principle of
adversarial litigation when it raised and determined issues which
were not pleaded before the trial court, or raised in the grounds of
appeal, and on which the appellant was not afforded an
opportunity to be heard. It is argued that a court's jurisdiction is
invoked and circumscribed by the pleadings; and the respondent's
case was prosecuted solely on the redundancy package.
3.3 It is submitted that by introducing and deciding the case on
unpleaded statutory and contractual claims, the court in effect
tried a case that was never presented, thus violating the
J7
appellant's right to a fair hearing under Article 18 of the
Constitution. Counsel relies on various authorities, including
Atlantic Bakery v ZESCO Limited3 and Murray & Roberts
Constructions Limited v Lusaka Premier Health Limited and
Another4 which emphatically state that a court cannot grant relief
,
that is not claimed or set up a new case for the parties.
3.4 With respect to ground 2, the appellant's brief argument is that the
court's interpretation of section 54(l)(c) is patently erroneous
because the provision expressly applies to a "contract of
employment of a fix~d duration." That a "permanent and
pensionable contract," as defined in section 3 of the Act, is
practically distinct, expiring only upon retirement unless
terminated and the court's failure to recognise this fundamental
distinction resulted in a flawed analysis of the provision.
3.5 Further still, counsel submits, the court ignored its own consistent
line of authority in Stanbic Bank Zambia Ltd v Natasha Patel1
and Midlands Milling Ltd v Lloyd Tembo2 where it held that
,
severance pay under section 54 is not available to dismissed
employees, particularly those on permanent contracts. This
departure from the principle of stare decisis, without justification)
it is argued, creates a grave uncertainty in the law.
J8
3.6 At the hearing of the appeal, Mr. Pasi, on behalf of the appellant,
recapped the written arguments and stressed the contradictory
line of authority from the Court of Appeal, ending in the decision
of the full bench in Kingfred Phiri v Life Master Ltd5 where the
,
court sought to correct the erroneous interpretation in the present
matter. Counsel urged us to endorse the decision in the latter case.
3.7 In response to ground 1, Mr. Chungu, counsel for the respondent,
submits that the claim for "service benefits," though imprecise due
to the respondent being unrepresented, was sufficiently pleaded;
that the Industrial Relations Division should prioritise substantial
justice over technical pleadings; and that a point of law could be
raised at any stage, hence the claim under section 54(1)(c) and
the fixed term contract.
3.8 As to ground 2, counsel submits that section 54(1)(c} applies to
permanent contracts, and that all employment contracts in
Zambia are "for a fixed duration!P since it is known that employees
on such contract will retire on reaching retirement age. He insists
that permanent contracts are not among the categories expressly
excluded by section 54(3) of the Act.
3.9 Because of the profound legal doubt created by the conflicting
judgments of the Court of Appeal, counsel urges us to provide an
J9
authoritative and final interpretation of section 54. He says this
is a matter of significant public importance affecting countless
employment relationships across the nation.
3 .10 In reply, Mr. Linyama the learned co-counsel for the appellant re
emphasises that the duty to do substantial justice does not licence
a court to become an advocate for a party or to construct a case
not presented by a party. Counsel maintains that the respondent's
pleadings and evidence were specific to the redundancy claim, and
that the introduction of new issues was procedurally erroneous.
4 Consideration of the Appeal and Decision
4.1 We have considered the record of appeal in its entirety and the
submissions by learned counsel for the parties and the numerous
authorities cited. This appeal presents us with an opportunity to
reaffirm first, principles of procedural law and to provide much
needed clarity on a contentious area of substantive employment
law. We propose to deal with the grounds of appeal seriatim.
4.2 With respect to ground 1, the appellant contends that the Court of
Appeal fundamentally erred by adjudicating upon issues not
pleaded before the trial court. We start by stating the obvious that
pleadings (in this case the complaint, answer, reply and affidavits)
are the essential, foundational documents in civil litigation that
JlO
define the scope of the dispute, facts and legal issues. We have
made this point very clear in a plethora of authorities.
4.3 In Anderson Kambela Mazoka & Others v Levy Patrick
Mwanawasa & Others6 we held that pleadings serve to define the
,
action between the parties, to give fair notice of the case that must
be met, and to set the boundaries within which judicial authority
may be exercised. A party cannot, at the trial of a cause, depart
from the case set up in their pleadings. Silumbwe v Barclays Bank
Zambia Limited7 cited by the appellant is another good example.
We said in that case that as a general rule, a party cannot rely on
matters which have not been pleaded.
4.4 The respondent's notice of complaint and supporting affidavit were
clear. As correctly argued by counsel for the appellant, his claim
for "service benefits" was explicitly and exclusively tied to the items
listed in the redundancy letter (Exhibit "ZJH 1"). The t

7 However, this did not mean that the respondent was entitled to the
service benefits under the fixed term contract that had expired
before he was offered employment on permanent basis or that he
J12
had not been paid gratuity at the end of the contract or any other
renewed contract.
4.8 Therefore, the Court of Appeal's decision to venture into the
benefits under the fixed-term contract/ s which expired before the
respondent was employed on permanent basis and into section
54(1)(c), was a grave error. It constituted a radical departure from
the case the respondent had pleaded and presented at the trial.
4.9 In Atlantic Bakery v ZESCO Limited3 we said a court should
,
confine its decisions to the questions raised in the pleadings; that
it can thus not grant relief which is not claimed; and that litigation
is for the parties, not the court. Mr. Chungu's invocation of the
principle that Industrial Relations Courts should administer
"substantial justice" is not a remedy for imperfect pleadings.
4.10 Substantial justice is justice according to law, administered fairly
to both parties. It does not empower a court to assume the role of
an advocate and craft a new cause of action of its own accord. As
properly submitted by counsel for the appellant, to do so, as
occurred in this matter, is a profound violation of the audi alteram
partem rule, a cornerstone of natural justice embedded in Article
18 of our Constitution of 2016.
J13
4.11 Quite clearly, the appellant was denied the right to adduce
evidence or make submissions on whether benefits under the
fixed-term contract had already been paid, or on the correct
interpretation of section 54 in the context of summary dismissal.
4.12 In Zambia National Commercial Bank PLC v Geoffrey
Muyamwa and 88 Others8 we clarified the jurisdiction and power
,
of the Industrial Relations Court on unpleaded matters and reliefs
vis-a-vis the court's power to grant such remedy as it considers
just and equitable and to make any other order or award as the
court may consider fit in the circumstances of the case. We shall
quote only a portion of the text at pages 16 to 17 of the appellant's
List of Authorities and Heads of Argument. It reads as follows:
" .... The decision we have made in the preceding paragraph follows
our consideration and dismissal of the argument by counsel for the
respondents that the court below was on firm ground when it
ordered payment of long service gratuity though it was not
specifically pleaded because the court below enjoys jurisdiction to
award any remedy it deems reasonable as per the Matale case and
due to the fact that it is a court of substantial justice. We have
dismissed this argument because, though it is true that the court
below is at large to award any remedy it deems suitable in doing
substantial justice, it must not be one-sided nor is it akin to trial
by ambush and must, therefore, not override the basic tenets of
justice by which a party is afforded a right to be heard on a remedy
sought by the opponent. Consequently, the respondents in this
matter can only invoke the principle of substantial justice on their
quest for an award of long service gratuity if they can show that
the appellant's right to be heard on. the claim has not been
breached. This, they should have done, by showing that they
pleaded long service gratuity and led evidence on it. In this case,
the record clearly shows that long service gratuity was not an issue
and as such not addressed by the appellant. The court below was,
therefore, not at large to award it."
J14
4.13 Furthermore, the submission that a point of law can be raised at
any time is not absolute. It applies to points arising from the
pleaded facts and issues, not to an entirely new factual and legal
basis for a claim. The alleged point of law, namely, the applicability
of section 54(1)(c) of the Act, was intricately linked to an
unpleaded claim regarding benefits under the fixed term contract.
4.14 We, therefore, find and hold that the Court of Appeal acted in
excess of its jurisdiction by determining issues that were not
pleaded before the trial court and not properly arising from the
grounds of appeal. Ground 1 of the appeal has merit and succeeds.
4.15 We come now to ground 2, concerning the interpretation of section
54(l)(c). The appellant contends that the Court of Appeal erred by
putting forward an interpretation of the law that is not only
erroneous but also plainly inconsistent with its own recent
jurisprudence. Although the success of ground 1 is determinative
of this appeal, the profound public importance of the legal issue
raised by this ground of appeal and the conflicting judgments of
the Court of Appeal compel us to provide definitive guidance.
4.16 The question is: Does section 54(1)(c) entitle an employee on a
permanent and pensionable contract, who has been summarily
dismissed for gross misconduct, to a severance pay? The principles
JlS
of statutory interpretation require us to ascertain the intention of
the legislature from the words used, read in their context and in
light of the statute's overall purpose and scheme.
4.17 Section 54( 1) begins with a general condition: "An employer shall
pay an employee a severance pay where the employee's contract of
employment is terminated or has expired". It then proceeds to
prescribe specific circumstances and computation methods.
Paragraph (c), on which the Court of Appeal relied, states precisely
"where a contract of employment of a fixed duration has been
terminated, severance pay shall be a gratuity at the rate of not less
than twenty-five percent of the employees' basic pay earned during
the contract period as at the effective date of termination."
4.18 Section 54(3) provides that "the severance pay under this section
shall not be paid to a casual employee, a temporary employee, an
employee engaged on a long-term contract or an employee serving
a period of probation."
4.19 The Act also provides definitions that are crucial to this a
